questions.text: Two modulation schemes sharing the same symbol rate and carrier frequency will generally have SCFs that:
choices: Peak at the same cycle-frequency locations, but with different shape/strength there
Peak at completely unrelated cycle frequencies
Are identical in every respect
questions.explanation: Cycle-frequency peak locations are set mainly by timing parameters (symbol rate, carrier, framing); the detailed shape/strength at those locations depends on the modulation scheme itself — that combination is the 'fingerprint' blind classifiers match against.

questions.text: Blind modulation classification using cyclic features works by:
choices: Comparing the observed SCF's peak locations and shape against known modulation signatures
Measuring only the signal's total received power
Requiring advance knowledge of which modulation was transmitted
questions.explanation: Cyclic-feature classification is fundamentally a pattern-matching problem over SCF structure, letting a receiver infer modulation type without prior knowledge of the transmission scheme.

questions.text: A cyclostationary detector can succeed at SNRs where an energy detector fails because:
choices: It tests for spectral correlation at a cycle frequency, which stationary noise essentially lacks, rather than testing total power
It amplifies the received signal before analysis
It always requires a stronger signal than an energy detector needs
questions.explanation: Energy detectors compare total power to a noise floor and lose at low SNR; cyclic detectors instead test for correlation between spectral components at a candidate cycle frequency — a property stationary noise essentially doesn't have, so even a weak signal's cyclic correlation can be distinguishable.

questions.text: Two signals that fully overlap in ordinary frequency can often still be separated using cyclic features because:
choices: They typically have different symbol/framing rates, giving distinct cycle-frequency peaks even where frequency spectra coincide
One signal is always physically located farther away
Cyclic separation only works when signals don't overlap in frequency
questions.explanation: Different clocking (symbol/framing) rates give different signals distinct cycle-frequency peaks, providing a separating axis that ordinary frequency-domain analysis structurally cannot offer when the signals coincide in frequency.

questions.text: Why is a stationary noise process a poor competitor against a cyclostationary detector, even at low SNR?
choices: Stationary processes have essentially no spectral redundancy — no correlation between spectral components separated by any nonzero cycle frequency
Stationary noise always has less total power than any real signal
Stationary processes cannot be represented in the frequency domain at all
questions.explanation: Spectral redundancy — correlation between frequency components spaced α apart — is essentially absent in stationary noise, which is exactly the property a cyclostationary detector tests for and exploits.

questions.text: One word: the general term for detecting an unknown/uncooperative transmitter's signal in a shared band, one of CSP's main real-world uses, is spectrum ___.
questions.explanation: Spectrum sensing — detecting occupied vs. free spectrum, often for cognitive-radio use cases — is one of the main applications of low-SNR cyclostationary detection.

questions.text: Two co-channel signals have symbol rates of 5000 and 12000 symbols/second. At how many distinct fundamental cycle frequencies (in Hz) would you expect their SCFs to show separate peaks (counting only the two fundamental rates, ignoring harmonics)?
questions.explanation: Each signal's fundamental cycle frequency equals its own symbol rate (5000 Hz and 12000 Hz), so there are 2 distinct fundamental peak locations — exactly the separating information ordinary frequency-domain analysis alone would miss, since both signals occupy the same frequency band.

questions.text: Cumulative review: the reason ordinary PSD analysis cannot distinguish the two overlapping co-channel signals from the previous question is that:
choices: The PSD is only the α = 0 slice of the SCF, so it cannot see structure at the nonzero cycle frequencies where the two signals actually differ
PSD analysis can only ever show one signal at a time regardless of overlap
The two signals must have identical power for PSD analysis to fail
questions.explanation: From cyclostationarity-core-theory: the ordinary PSD is structurally restricted to α = 0, so any information that only shows up at nonzero cycle frequencies — like two signals' different symbol rates — is invisible to it no matter how carefully it's estimated.

questions.text: Which of these is NOT one of the three applications covered in this module?
choices: Estimating a signal's carrier frequency from its DC offset
Blind modulation classification using cyclic fingerprints
Low-SNR signal detection via spectral redundancy
questions.explanation: This module covers modulation classification, low-SNR/spectrum-sensing detection, and separation of overlapping co-channel signals — carrier-frequency estimation from DC offset isn't one of them (and isn't a meaningful CSP technique at all: DC offset carries no cyclic information).
